items.title: Global synchronized bond selloff: four countries, four central banks, one trade — 'get me out of duration'
items.idea: Barclays' Ajay Rajadhyaksha: 'Last week, long bonds broke. Not in one country. In all of them. Simultaneously. Four countries. Four different political systems. Four different central banks. But the same trade — get me out of duration.' The trigger is Iran war oil shock compounding fiscal overextension. Japan and Germany long-term breakevens are surging despite having appeared to solve their inflation problems. The 10Y Treasury yield now exceeds the S&P 500 earnings yield to an extent not seen since the tail end of the dot-com bust in early 2002.
items.angle: The 60/40 hasn't broken yet and equities are barely moving, but the earnings yield vs bond yield inversion hasn't been this severe since 2002. Iran removed the Fed's escape hatch for rescuing the mismatch.
items.tweetA: Barclays: 'Last week, long bonds broke. Not in one country. In all of them. Simultaneously.' Four countries. Four central banks. One trade. The 10Y Treasury now yields MORE than the S&P 500 earnings yield — last time we saw this was the 2002 dot-com bust. Equities are barely down. Either bonds are wrong or stocks are next.
items.tweetB: Robin Brooks at Brookings calls the global bond selloff a 'healthy repricing.' Barclays calls it simple and uncomfortable: too much debt, too little fiscal discipline, no political appetite to fix it. Iran added an oil supply shock on top. The Fed has no escape hatch this time. $TLT $SPY

charts.desc: Bloomberg charts: global bond yield synchronization across US/Japan/UK/France; Japan + Germany breakeven inflation surging; 60/40 portfolio resilience; ERP (earnings yield vs 10Y) at 2002 levels; UK gilt 10Y intraday swings on political news. The ERP chart alone is worth saving.

items.title: 10Y at 4.6% 52-week high; rate HIKE odds at 45%; Warsh inherits stagflation Fed at June 16-17 FOMC
items.idea: Kalshi/CME markets are pricing ~45% odds of a rate HIKE in 2026 with June pause at 96%. Warsh's first FOMC as Fed Chair is June 16-17. He inherits a 4.6% 10Y Treasury (new 52-week high), 30Y above 5.13%, oil at $102, and Empire State Manufacturing at +19.6 — highest since April 2022. The stagflation setup is fully assembled: strong manufacturing, elevated energy prices, rising yields, and a new Fed Chair with no political capital to deploy.
items.angle: Warsh is walking into the worst possible inheritance — a market pricing hikes, a supply shock from Iran, and a fiscal deficit that makes rate cuts politically impossible. His first meeting sets the tone for the entire year.
items.tweetA: Warsh's first FOMC: June 16-17. He inherits a 4.6% 10Y, 30Y above 5.13%, oil at $102, and Empire State Manufacturing at its highest since April 2022. Markets are pricing 45% odds of a rate HIKE in 2026. There is no soft landing here. There is a new Fed Chair with an impossible inbox.
items.tweetB: 45% hike odds. 96% June pause. Empire State Mfg at a 4-year high. Iran keeping oil at $102. This is the stagflation setup the Fed has been pretending wouldn't happen. Warsh hasn't signaled anything yet. June 16 is when the market finds out if he blinks. $TBT $RINF

items.title: Trump cancels Iran strike at Gulf monarchs' request; oil drops $2 but Hormuz structural issue unchanged
items.idea: Saudi MBS, Qatari Emir, and UAE MbZ all called Trump directly Monday morning to request the Iran strike be cancelled. Trump: 'Hold off for two or three days... very close to making a deal.' Iran submitted a response via Pakistan mediator. Brent peaked above $112 intraday, settled below $110. WTI from $104+ to ~$102.50. US military has 'redirected' 85 ships since the Iran port blockade began. The bond market did not react — 10Y held at 4.6%.
items.angle: The Gulf monarchs calling Trump directly is the tell: they are more exposed to Hormuz disruption than anyone. The strike pause bought days. The structural blockade and oil supply shock are unchanged.
items.tweetA: Trump called off the Iran strike. Oil fell $2. Brent: $112 to $110. The bond market didn't care — 10Y held at 4.6%, new 52-week high. 85 ships still rerouted. Saudi, Qatar, UAE bought Trump two or three days. The Hormuz supply shock is not resolved. Just paused. $OIL $BNO
items.tweetB: Saudi MBS, Qatari Emir, UAE MbZ all personally called Trump to cancel the Iran strike. Three heads of state on the phone Monday morning. That's the tell — Gulf monarchies are terrified of escalation. 'Very close to a deal' has a shelf life measured in news cycles. Oil at $102 is your floor if Hormuz stays closed.

items.title: Trump: Taiwan should 'cool it a little bit' — signals US defense commitment is negotiable; Xi invokes Thucydides Trap
items.idea: At the Beijing summit, Trump said: 'China is a very, very powerful, big country. That's a very small island. Think of it, it's 59 miles away. We're 9,500 miles away. Taiwan would be very smart to cool it a little bit.' Xi invoked the Thucydides Trap at the summit. Brookings' Ryan Hass: 'Trump's public openness to negotiating with Beijing over Taiwan will serve as the diplomatic equivalent of a matador waving a red flag in front of a bull.' Rare earths were reportedly not even mentioned in the talks.
items.angle: Taiwan is the single-node bottleneck for the entire AI buildout — TSMC produces all advanced AI silicon. If Taiwan's status becomes genuinely negotiable, no AI capex model is using the right discount rate.
items.tweetA: Trump at the Beijing summit: 'China is a very powerful country. Taiwan is a very small island, 59 miles away. We are 9,500 miles away. Taiwan would be very smart to cool it.' Xi responded with the Thucydides Trap. The AI buildout runs through TSMC. The world's most important fab just became a negotiating chip. $TSM $NVDA
items.tweetB: Brookings: 'Trump's openness to negotiate over Taiwan is the diplomatic equivalent of a matador waving a red flag.' TSMC produces 100% of advanced AI silicon. Hyperscalers have committed $1T+ in capex on the assumption this supply chain is stable. That assumption just became a variable. $TSM $AMD $NVDA

charts.desc: Bloomberg chart: Taiwanese stocks surged with AI boom but gave a tepid response to the summit — suggesting the market is beginning to price geopolitical risk into the island's valuation premium. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: China April data: investment back to decline, exports can't offset weak consumption; US ag imports fell 65% as Beijing diversified
items.idea: China's April data showed growth slowing broadly: investment returned to decline, booming exports are no longer sufficient to offset weakening domestic consumption. US agricultural shipments to China fell from $24B in 2024 to $8.3B in 2025 as Beijing sought alternative suppliers. China's imports from the rest of the world rose 26% YoY in the first four months while imports from the US fell 11%. Beijing now faces a binary choice: large-scale domestic stimulus (Xi has long resisted) or push Iran to reopen Hormuz (politically painful).
items.angle: The '$17B ag deal' Trump came home with is Beijing giving back a fraction of the $15.7B it redirected elsewhere. The power balance is shifting faster than consensus imagined, but China is also quietly hurting.
items.tweetA: China April data: investment back to decline. Exports can't offset weak consumption. US ag shipments fell from $24B (2024) to $8.3B (2025) as Beijing diversified. China's imports from the rest of world rose 26% while US imports fell 11%. The $17B ag deal Trump came home with is Beijing handing back a fraction of what it rerouted. $FXI
items.tweetB: Beijing's post-summit choice: large-scale domestic stimulus (Xi hates it) or pressure Iran to reopen Hormuz (politically costly). Either way, the 'China won the summit' narrative ignores an economy cracking internally. Investment declining. Consumption weak. Export-led growth running on borrowed time. Watch April industrial profits. $MCHI

charts.desc: Bloomberg charts: US ag exports to China collapsing across all major product categories; broader trend showing China diversification away from US suppliers. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: The Cheap Chip Trap: NVIDIA Vera Rubin at 19.5 TFLOPs/W vs Trainium 3 at 2.5 — the ASIC procurement model is broken
items.idea: Morgan Stanley slide 17 from their NVIDIA preview: Vera Rubin at 19.5 TFLOPs/watt on NVFP4 vs Amazon Trainium 3 at 2.5 TFLOPs/watt on FP8. The comparison is not apples-to-apples — NVIDIA designed Vera Rubin for FP4, the next-generation frontier inference precision, while Trainium 3 was designed for FP8. Even on FP8 alone, Vera Rubin outperforms Trainium 3 by multiples. Trainium 4 and TPUv8 won't support FP4 until 2027 at earliest. Custom ASICs win on procurement spreadsheets but commit the buyer to the same megawatt with dramatically fewer tokens for the asset's operational life.
items.angle: The grid constraint makes this permanent: ERCOT paused new interconnect studies, PJM queue runs into 2030s. The megawatt you have today is every megawatt you have for the decade. Jensen confirmed the 24/7 duty cycle at Dell World: 'billions of AI agents working 24/7.'
items.tweetA: NVIDIA Vera Rubin: 19.5 TFLOPs/watt. Amazon Trainium 3: 2.5. That is a 7.8x gap — and Rubin is measured at FP4 (next-gen workload) while Trainium 3 is measured at FP8 (what it was designed for). Custom silicon is cheaper to buy. Not to run. $NVDA $AMZN $GOOGL
items.tweetB: The cheap chip trap: ASICs save you on procurement. But ERCOT paused new interconnect studies. PJM queue runs to 2030s. The megawatt you have today is every megawatt you have for the decade. Trainium 3 at 2.5 TFLOPs/W vs Vera Rubin at 19.5 means you didn't buy a cheaper chip. You committed your grid to a token deficit for five years. $NVDA

charts.desc: CHART WORTH SAVING: Morgan Stanley slide 17 — TFLOPs/watt comparison bar chart: Vera Rubin (NVFP4) at top at 19.5, TPUv7 in the middle, Amazon Trainium 3 at bottom at 2.5. The chart tells the entire compute strategy story for 2025-2028 in one visual.

items.title: Samsung 45,000-worker strike May 21 — largest semiconductor work stoppage in history; warm-down already started
items.idea: Samsung Electronics' 45,000 workers are set to strike May 21 for 18 days — the largest semiconductor work stoppage in history. The warm-down of fab operations has already started ahead of the strike. Estimated financial impact: 30-100 trillion won. Seagate (STX) already fell 7% on AI memory supply concerns and Samsung strike fears. Micron -3%, Nvidia/Broadcom/Intel all red. This activates into NVDA earnings Wednesday, where HBM is a critical input to GB200 NVL rack density.
items.angle: SOX is 32% above its 50DMA — the most stretched sector in the market — and a major supply shock is activating the same week as the binary NVDA print. Long vol here is asymmetrically cheap.
items.tweetA: 45,000 Samsung workers strike May 21 for 18 days. Largest semi work stoppage in history. Warm-down already started. NVDA reports Wednesday. HBM is a direct input to GB200 rack density. The market hasn't priced a 30-100 trillion won supply disruption into the most binary print of Q2. $NVDA $STX $MU
items.tweetB: $STX -7% on Samsung strike fears. $MU -3%. SOX at 32% above 50DMA — most stretched sector in the market. Binary NVDA print Wednesday. This is the setup: the most overextended sector, a supply shock activating, and a single earnings event that can move the entire tape. Long vol. $SOXS

items.title: SpaceX S-1 expected Wednesday May 20 — $75B raise is 3x the largest US IPO ever; ticker SPCX, trading June 12
items.idea: SpaceX is expected to file its S-1 Wednesday May 20 at a $1.75T-$2T valuation. The $75B raise would be 3x larger than the previous record (Saudi Aramco at $29B, $1.7T valuation). Probable Nasdaq ticker: SPCX. Pricing as early as June 11, trading June 12. With Musk's OpenAI lawsuit resolved Monday and xAI also in the pipeline, two of the largest private AI companies simultaneously have clearer IPO paths. The market must absorb this into a backdrop of 45% hike odds, oil at $102, and 10Y at 4.6%.
items.angle: Three record IPOs in the pipeline (SpaceX, OpenAI, xAI) = the largest equity supply event in history hitting a market already stretched on rates and geopolitics.
items.tweetA: SpaceX S-1 expected Wednesday. $75B raise. Aramco's record was $29B at $1.7T. SpaceX is doing 2.6x that at $1.75-2T valuation. Ticker: SPCX. Trading June 12. OpenAI's IPO path is now clear after Monday's verdict. The largest dual-IPO supply event in history is loading. Who absorbs this? $NDAQ
items.tweetB: Market debating NVDA earnings. SpaceX is about to file an S-1 for the largest IPO in history — $75B, 3x Aramco. This hits a market with 45% hike odds, oil at $102, 10Y at 4.6%, and a Samsung-induced semi supply shock. The IPO supply-demand math into June is brutal for risk assets.

items.title: Goldman Sachs 13F: exits XRP + SOL ETFs, cuts ETH 70%, holds $700M BTC — institutional crypto = BTC only
items.idea: Goldman Sachs Q1 13F filing: exited XRP ETF positions entirely, exited SOL ETF positions entirely, cut ETH exposure by 70%, and maintained $700M in BTC ETFs. This happened the same week as: BTC dropped to $76,400 (lowest since late April), ETH fell below $2,113 (-3.37% session), total crypto market lost ~$1.07B, and the 6-week ETF inflow streak snapped. Smart money index and whale wallet data from Coinbase both showing lean sell signals.
items.angle: Goldman's 13F is a public data point confirming the BTC-only institutional rotation thesis. Combined with Aschenbrenner's move (item 11), this is two very different actors converging on the same trade at the same time.
items.tweetA: Goldman Sachs 13F: exited XRP + SOL ETFs entirely. Cut ETH 70%. Holds $700M BTC. The largest institutional crypto holder is publicly going BTC-only. This is not a bear signal on crypto — it's the clearest confirmation yet that institutional adoption means Bitcoin, not 'the crypto space.' $BTC $ETH $SOL
items.tweetB: Goldman exits XRP, SOL, cuts ETH 70%, holds BTC. ETH at $2,113 breaking critical support. 6-week ETF inflow streak snapped. Bitcoin whale wallets leaning sell per Coinbase. This is institutional rotation, not capitulation. BTC at $76K is the accumulation zone for the BTC-only thesis.

items.title: Iran launches 'Hormuz Safe' — BTC-backed shipping insurance for the Strait; first kinetic-conflict BTC financial product
items.idea: Iran launched 'Hormuz Safe' — a BTC-backed insurance product for shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. A sanctioned state is creating a parallel financial product in Bitcoin to route around USD-denominated international insurance markets. This is the first time a kinetic conflict has generated direct, structured demand for BTC as a financial instrument rather than as a store of value or speculative asset.
items.angle: This is a category-defining event for BTC. Not speculative demand — operational demand. A state-backed financial product denominated in Bitcoin. Watch for premium formation in BTC shipping markets.
items.tweetA: Iran just launched 'Hormuz Safe' — BTC-backed insurance for shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. A sanctioned state building a parallel financial system in Bitcoin to route around USD insurance markets. Geopolitical BTC demand just went from theoretical to operational. $BTC
items.tweetB: Hormuz is physically blocked. 85 US ships rerouted. Iran is now issuing BTC-denominated shipping insurance. This is the first time a kinetic conflict has generated structured BTC financial product demand. The petrodollar system is being stress-tested in real time, and BTC is being offered as the alternative. $BTC

items.title: Ex-OpenAI Aschenbrenner: short NVDA/AMD, buying BTC miners as $13.6B AI power infrastructure play
items.idea: Ex-OpenAI safety researcher Leopold Aschenbrenner is short NVDA and AMD while buying BTC miners as an AI infrastructure play, sizing this at $13.6B. His thesis: BTC miners own the megawatt — stranded/cheap power, purpose-built cooling infrastructure, physical rack capacity — at dramatic discount valuations vs pure AI names. The GPU premium is in the chip; the durable moat in AI infrastructure is in the power.
items.angle: This converges with the Cheap Chip Trap thesis (item 6) and Goldman's BTC accumulation (item 9) into one coherent framework: power is the scarce asset, not silicon. Miners own power at discount.
items.tweetA: Ex-OpenAI Aschenbrenner: short $NVDA $AMD, long BTC miners for a $13.6B AI infrastructure play. His thesis: miners own the megawatt. Stranded energy, purpose-built cooling, rack density — all at discount valuations vs pure AI names. The GPU premium is in the chip. The moat is in the power. $MARA $RIOT $CLSK
items.tweetB: The BTC miner as AI infrastructure play: ERCOT paused interconnect studies. PJM queue to 2030s. Miners already have the power, the cooling, the physical plant. NVDA is priced for perfection. The power layer is not. Aschenbrenner sees $13.6B here. This is the Cheap Chip Trap from the supply side. $MARA $CORZ

items.title: OpenAI wins Musk lawsuit in 2 hours; $134B claim collapses; IPO path cleared — Parekh's contrarian: Musk won the forum
items.idea: The jury returned a verdict in under 2 hours after 3 weeks of testimony. Statute of limitations: Musk already knew about the actions he claims were unjust by 2020 (his own X post: 'OpenAI is essentially captured by Microsoft'). The $134B claim collapsed, and the judge confirmed the OpenAI restructuring stands — clearing the path for the IPO. The judge cast doubt on remaining antitrust claims ('there is a lot of competition' in AI). Parekh's contrarian read: for Musk, this was a PR forum for hammering Altman publicly ahead of the SpaceX/xAI mega-IPO, at the cost of lawyer fees.
items.angle: Both OpenAI and SpaceX now have simultaneous IPO clearance — the largest dual-IPO supply event in history is now loading. The antitrust claims still pending are the tail risk.
items.tweetA: Two hours. Three weeks of testimony. Musk loses OpenAI. The jury was persuaded by his own 2020 X post: 'OpenAI is essentially captured by Microsoft.' Statute of limitations. $134B claim gone. Restructuring stands. IPO clock starts now for the company that went from $3B to $30B ARR in one year. $MSFT
items.tweetB: Parekh's read: Musk won a PR forum ahead of SpaceX/xAI's mega-IPO. Spent 3 weeks publicly hammering Altman on his own platform. The lawyer fees were a marketing spend. He'll appeal to keep the narrative alive through roadshow season. Never interrupt an enemy when they're building your next audience.

items.title: Context rot research: 18 SOTA models all degrade with longer input; accuracy collapses past 50% context window
items.idea: Chroma researchers tested 18 state-of-the-art models (Claude, GPT, Gemini) on simple retrieval tasks at different input lengths. Every single model performed worse as context grew. Some models scoring near 100% on short inputs dropped below 50% on the identical task with longer context. Key mechanisms: 'lost in the middle' attention bias, distractor confusion at scale, positional sensitivity, vocabulary mismatch. Anthropic's internal principle: 'good context engineering means finding the smallest possible set of high signal tokens that maximise the likelihood of the outcome you want.' LLM accuracy declines noticeably past 50% context window capacity; Anthropic's auto-compaction only triggers at 95%.
items.angle: Context window marketing (4K to 10M tokens = 250-2500x in 4 years) is a spec war. The actual performance envelope is much narrower. Any agentic workflow burning through context is operating in degraded mode most of the time.
items.tweetA: Chroma tested 18 SOTA models on simple retrieval. Every single one got worse as context grew. Some dropped from near 100% to below 50% on the identical task. The race to 10M token context windows is a marketing war. Degradation starts at 50% of the advertised window. Context rot is real and unsolved.
items.tweetB: LLM accuracy degrades noticeably past 50% context window capacity. Anthropic's auto-compaction triggers at 95%. So by the time you see the compression message, you've been running in degraded mode for half your session. The 1M token context is a ceiling, not a performance target. Agent workflows are losing quality they don't know they're losing.

items.title: UK PM Starmer fatally wounded; Andy Burnham as probable successor; gilts at 5% with Liz Truss PTSD
items.idea: PM Starmer faces calls for resignation from 80+ Labour MPs following dismal local election results. Andy Burnham (Manchester mayor) is now the frontrunner. Burnham previously said the government shouldn't be 'in hock to the bond market' — UK 10Y gilts immediately breached 5% on the political news. He walked back within 24 hours, pledging to maintain Labour's conservative fiscal rules. The ghost of Liz Truss looms: a PM who ignored bond markets in 2022 lasted 44 days.
items.angle: UK political risk = UK gilt risk = global contagion risk in a world where the bond market is already in slow-motion crash mode.
items.tweetA: UK 10Y gilts breached 5%. Starmer is done — 80+ MPs calling for resignation. Andy Burnham literally said the government shouldn't be 'in hock to the bond market.' The market immediately moved. He walked it back in 24 hours. This is 2022 Liz Truss PTSD playing out in real time with zero margin for error. $GBPUSD
items.tweetB: Every credible UK PM candidate must promise a better NHS, more growth, no austerity — and submit to the bond market. Burnham inherits 5%+ gilts, stagnant economy, no borrowing room. The next PM is Liz Truss in waiting. The only question is how long before the market finds out. $GBPUSD

charts.desc: Bloomberg chart: UK 10Y gilt yield intraday swings mapped against each political development. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: 2026 wealth step functions: $700K = middle class trap; $6-8M = first level of 'rich'; $100M+ = walking institution
items.idea: BowTied Bull's 2026 framework maps 7 wealth levels by lifestyle impact. Key steps: $700K = 'middle class trap' — illiquid in house and retirement accounts, still time-trapped. $3-4M = first real lifestyle change (frequency and consistency impossible to fake). $6-8M = first level of actually 'rich' ($4-6M liquid at 5% = $200-300K without working, no alarm clock, fully location-agnostic, variable expenses irrelevant). $15-20M = post-economic. $100M+ = walking institution, family office territory. The key insight: income has nothing to do with wealth — 'rich' is defined by what you earn doing nothing.
items.angle: The real inflection for portfolio strategy is around $6-8M liquid: that's when volatility management, tax planning, and asset protection become more important than return-maximization.
items.tweetA: BowTied Bull's 2026 wealth map: $700K is still the middle class trap — illiquid in house and 401K, time-trapped. $3-4M is the first lifestyle jump (frequency is impossible to fake). $6-8M is the first level of actually rich — no alarm clock, no variable expense anxiety, fully location-agnostic. Most people are optimizing the wrong number.
items.tweetB: The most underrated wealth insight: income has nothing to do with wealth. Rich is defined by what you earn doing nothing. By that definition, most high earners are broke — house-rich, retirement-account-illiquid, time-trapped. The real step function is $6-8M liquid. Not $1M. Not $3M. $6-8M.

items.title: Philippines call center employment doubled in a decade — AI has not disrupted offshore services labor
items.idea: Apollo's Torsten Slok highlighted that Philippines call center/BPO employment grew by +2M since 2016, doubling in a decade. The Philippines employs ~1.3M+ in BPO/call centers, roughly 8% of GDP. This is concrete evidence that AI has not yet disrupted offshore services labor despite years of predictions. If the disruption comes as a sudden wave rather than a gradual trend (as agentic AI improves), it would be a sovereign economic shock — not just a labor market adjustment.
items.angle: The 'AI is replacing jobs now' narrative runs 10 years behind the data in services. But agentic AI breaking out of chat into autonomous task completion is the actual trigger to watch — and that inflection may be 2026-2027.
items.tweetA: Philippines call center employment: +2M since 2016. Doubled in a decade. Apollo's Slok points to this as evidence AI has not disrupted services labor. The Philippines BPO sector is ~8% of GDP. If the disruption comes as a wave rather than a trend, this is a sovereign economic shock, not a labor market adjustment.
items.tweetB: The AI job displacement thesis: where is it? Philippines BPO employment doubled since 2016. Services labor growing globally. Either displacement comes suddenly — one model generation that handles autonomous task completion — or the models are not yet capable at scale. Agentic AI in 2026-2027 is the real test.
